payload.text:
You are a senior UI/UX designer and data visualization engineer.

Create a REAL-TIME Gas Analytics Dashboard that EXACTLY COPIES the visual layout, card placement, spacing, and hierarchy of a modern HR Analytics dashboard reference, but remapped for gas and environmental monitoring data.

DO NOT change the layout structure.
ONLY replace labels, titles, and metrics with gas analytics equivalents.

----------------------------------
OVERALL DESIGN STYLE
----------------------------------
• Dark purple gradient background
• Glassmorphism card design
• Rounded rectangular cards
• Soft shadows and glow effects
• High-contrast white text
• Clean, professional industrial-monitoring aesthetic
• Desktop-first responsive layout

----------------------------------
HEADER SECTION
----------------------------------
• Large dashboard title on the left:
  "REAL-TIME GAS ANALYTICS DASHBOARD"

• Pill-shaped filter buttons on the right:
  - All Sensors
  - NH₃
  - CO₂
  - NOx
  - SO₂
  - Environment

----------------------------------
TOP KPI CARDS (SINGLE ROW – 5 CARDS)
----------------------------------
Create five large statistic cards with identical size and spacing:

1. NH₃ Level (ppm)
2. CO₂ Level (ppm)
3. NOx Level (ppb)
4. SO₂ Level (ppb)
5. System Temperature (°C)

Each card must display:
• Small label on top
• Large live-updating numeric value
• Smooth animation on value change

----------------------------------
MIDDLE ROW (3-COLUMN LAYOUT)
----------------------------------

LEFT PANEL (DONUT / PIE CHART)
Title: "Gas Concentration Share"
• Represents relative proportions of:
  - NH₃
  - CO₂
  - NOx
  - SO₂
• Legend aligned inside the card

CENTER PANEL (VERTICAL BAR CHART)
Title: "Gas Levels Over Time"
• Time-series vertical bars
• X-axis: Time
• Y-axis: Concentration
• Color-coded bars per gas type

RIGHT PANEL (SCROLLABLE TABLE)
Title: "Live Sensor Readings"
Columns:
• Timestamp
• NH₃ (ppm)
• CO₂ (ppm)
• NOx (ppb)
• SO₂ (ppb)
• Temperature (°C)
• Humidity (%)
• Weight (kg)

----------------------------------
LOWER LEFT PANEL (HORIZONTAL BAR CHART)
----------------------------------
Title: "Highest Average Gas Levels"
• Horizontal bars ranked by magnitude
• Categories:
  - NH₃
  - CO₂
  - NOx
  - SO₂

----------------------------------
BOTTOM ROW – DONUT CHART SUMMARY
----------------------------------
Create five small donut charts aligned horizontally:

1. NH₃ Safety Status
2. CO₂ Safety Status
3. NOx Safety Status
4. SO₂ Safety Status
5. Humidity Status

Each donut shows:
• Central numeric value
• Safety segmentation:
  - Green: Safe
  - Yellow: Warning
  - Red: Critical

----------------------------------
DATA & BEHAVIOR
----------------------------------
• All values are REAL-TIME
• Data streamed from ESP32 via API / MQTT / WebSocket
• Auto-refresh without page reload
• Live animations on charts
• Graceful handling of disconnected sensors

----------------------------------
COLOR RULES
----------------------------------
• NH₃ – Cyan
• CO₂ – Blue
• NOx – Orange
• SO₂ – Red
• Temperature/Humidity – Teal
• Maintain strong contrast

----------------------------------
IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS
----------------------------------
• Do NOT introduce additional panels
• Do NOT remove any sections
• Maintain original layout proportions
• Focus on readability and real-time performance

**The attached image is the reference of the analytics dashboard**

**Defining the Application's Purpose**

I'm now zeroing in on the core request: building a real-time gas analytics dashboard. I'm focusing on the React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS aspects, keeping in mind the visual reference provided, particularly the HR analytics dashboard layout (purple gradient, glassmorphism). I'm starting to visualize the structure.


**Outlining Key Dashboard Features**

I'm now detailing the specific data points: gas concentrations (NH3, CO2, NOx, SO2), temperature, humidity, and weight. The dashboard will feature real-time data simulation and auto-refresh. I'm planning animations and charts: Donut, Vertical Bar, Horizontal Bar, Table, and a summary Donut chart. The tech stack includes React 18+, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Recharts, and Lucide React. Design-wise, I'm sticking with a dark purple gradient, glassmorphism cards, and white text, along with a specific color scheme for gas readings.



**Structuring the Data Flow**

I'm now focusing on the application's core elements and data flow. I'm finalizing the component structure: the center will hold a `BarChart.tsx`, the right will feature `LiveReadingsTable.tsx`, and the bottom-left will utilize `HighestLevelsBarChart.tsx`. I've outlined `SafetyDonut.tsx` as the component for the bottom row. I'm also preparing `types.ts` for data interfaces and `constants.ts` to implement specific colors and threshold configurations. The critical part is the `dataService.ts`, where I'm working on a function to simulate ESP32 data generation and a custom hook to manage data streams.


**Refining Component Architecture**

I'm now detailing component design. I've chosen `data` in the App component for state management. I've designed `Header`, `KpiCard`, `GasConcentrationPie`, `GasLevelsBarChart`, `LiveReadingsTable`, `HighestLevelsBarChart`, and `SafetyDonut`. The `KpiCard` will have a subtle glow effect, while the `LiveReadingsTable` will have custom scrollbar styling. I'm leaning toward a `BarChart` for `GasLevelsBarChart`, showing the latest intervals, keeping time-series data clean and color-coded.


**Revising Layout Specifications**

I'm now iterating on the dashboard's layout, specifically the grid system. I've re-imagined the structure based on the reference: 5 KPI cards in the top row, a middle section with three columns, and five small donut charts at the bottom. The middle section will now feature a left column with both a pie chart and a horizontal bar chart. The central column houses the vertical bar chart, and the right column will hold the table. I'm choosing CSS Grid to handle the rows and columns.


**Implementing Visual Enhancements**

I'm now focusing on the visual refinements. I've settled on a glassmorphism style, using `bg-white/10` or `bg-slate-900/40`, `backdrop-blur-md`, `border border-white/10`, and `rounded-xl`. Text color will be either `text-white` or `text-slate-200`.

\n\n

**Addressing Performance Considerations**

I'm now resolving potential performance issues related to real-time updates. I'll use `useEffect` with `setInterval` to manage data updates, pushing new readings to a state array, ensuring to limit the array size to prevent browser memory issues. I'll make sure Recharts components are responsive. Lucide icons are in use, and I'm ensuring strict adherence to the defined color palette. Finally, I have begun drafting the state management in the App.tsx file.


**Finalizing Dashboard Structure**

I'm now fully immersed in the component assembly. I am structuring the layout: A top row for KPIs, the middle section as described before, and a bottom row. The focus now is on the Middle Row components. The left column will now feature a stacked view, a pie chart on top and a horizontal bar chart below. I'm focusing on responsiveness and implementing the correct component mappings. I'm also ensuring the correct type definitions.


**Confirming Implementation Details**

I'm now integrating the generated code, focusing on refining details and addressing the prompt's specifics. I'm double-checking Tailwind class usage for consistent spacing, especially `gap-4` and `p-4`. I'm also ensuring the correct directory structure with a "metadata.json" file and am using `React.StrictMode` and `createRoot`. I'm confirming there are no CSS files. The bottom row will be a container for the horizontal donuts. Finally, I'm validating the dark purple gradient, glassmorphism, the real-time simulation, and TypeScript implementation against the instructions.

payload.text:
Add this:

Create a real-time IoT dashboard that displays the operational status (ON/OFF) of sensors, actuators, and the ESP32 device using data from Firebase Realtime Database. The data is transmitted by an ESP32 microcontroller.

The dashboard must visually indicate whether each sensor/device is active, transmitting data, or inactive, including the controller itself.

Firebase Data Source:

Use Firebase Realtime Database as the backend data source. The dashboard must listen to live changes and update automatically without page refresh.

Devices / Sensors to Display:

Controller:
- ESP32 Operational Status

Gas Sensors:
- MQ-137 Sensor
- MQ-135 Sensor

Environmental Sensor:
- SCD41 (CO₂, Temperature, Humidity)

Weight Measurement:
- Load Cell

Actuators / Output Devices:
- Servo Motor
- UV-C Light
- LED Indicators (UV-C Status Based)

Power Monitoring:
- Battery Percentage

Functional Requirements:

1. Each device/sensor must have its own status card or row.
2. Display:
   - Device name
   - Status text (ON / OFF)
   - LED-style visual indicator
3. Status must update in real time from Firebase.

ESP32 Operational Status Logic:

The ESP32 status must be determined using a heartbeat or last-update timestamp sent to Firebase.

Example Firebase Field:

/iot_system/esp32_last_update: 1707645300

Dashboard Interpretation:

- If the last update timestamp is within a defined threshold (e.g., ≤ 15 seconds) → ESP32 Status = ON
- If no update or timestamp exceeds threshold → ESP32 Status = OFF

Alternative simple structure:

/iot_system/esp32_status: true

Where:
- true = ON
- false = OFF

Example Firebase Structure:

/iot_system/
    esp32_status: true
    esp32_last_update: 1707645300

    mq137: 12.5
    mq135: 8.3

    scd41:
        co2: 520
        temperature: 30.2
        humidity: 75

    loadcell: 1.25
    servo: true
    uvc: true
    battery: 78

Status Interpretation:

- ESP32 → ON if heartbeat/status = true
- MQ Sensors → ON if value > 0
- SCD41 → ON if CO₂ data exists
- Load Cell → ON if weight reading exists
- Servo → ON if true / active
- UV-C → ON if true
- Battery → Always display percentage

UV-C & LED Indicator Logic:

- If UV-C = ON
    → RED LED = ON
    → GREEN LED = OFF

- If UV-C = OFF
    → RED LED = OFF
    → GREEN LED = ON

Battery Widget:

Display:
- Battery percentage
- Battery icon / progress bar

Color Coding:
- Green = 60–100%
- Yellow = 30–59%
- Red = 0–29%

Visual Design Requirements:

- Card or grid layout
- LED circle indicators:
    Green = ON
    Red = OFF
- Include icons per device
- Fully responsive dashboard

Real-Time Binding Requirement:

Use Firebase Realtime Database listeners (onValue / live snapshot listener) to automatically update the UI whenever ESP32 uploads new readings or heartbeat signals.

**Defining the Requirement**

I've started by dissecting the request. The core objective is to integrate a "Real-time IoT Dashboard" within the established "SolaniRad Analytic Dashboard". The envisioned functionality centers on displaying operational statuses, specifically the ON/OFF state of devices.


**Detailing the Devices**

I'm now diving into the specific devices and sensors for the IoT integration. I've broken them down into categories like controllers (ESP32), gas sensors, environmental sensors, weight sensors, actuators, indicators, and power sources. I am outlining specific logic for each device, like how the ESP32's status will be determined by either heartbeat or timestamp, and outlining how I will derive 'ON' status and device state. I am starting to consider the visuals like cards, LED circles, and icons as well, but this is less fleshed out. The tech stack is targeted to be React, Tailwind CSS, and Firebase Realtime Database.


**Adapting the Data Structure**

I'm now focusing on adapting to the data structure changes. Specifically, I'm refactoring the application to accommodate nested data from Firebase, as opposed to the flat structure I was initially using. I am defining a new `SystemStatus` interface for clarity. Furthermore, I've outlined the new `StatusPanel.tsx` component to present device statuses, battery levels, and indicators, computing "Active/Inactive" states. I am also planning updates to `App.tsx`, focusing on the `onValue` listener to accommodate the nested data and calculate ESP32 heartbeat status, plus integrate the new panel and ensure existing sensor reading mappings function correctly.


**Reconciling Data Structures**

I'm currently working to reconcile Firebase's data format with existing application components. I've drafted a `SystemData` interface to mirror the incoming data, including ESP32 status, sensor readings, and other device states. The focus is to map this new data to the established `SensorReading` type, ensuring chart compatibility, and create a specific component for displaying the "ON/OFF" statuses of devices. I'm building an `IoTStatusGrid.tsx` component to handle this specific function. I am considering the use of the `lastHeartbeat` timestamp as well.


**Mapping the Readings**

I'm now zeroing in on data mapping within `App.tsx`, and making some critical assumptions. For the MQ-137, I'm mapping it to NH3, while MQ-135 will be mapped to NOx/SO2 (for existing chart compatibility). CO2 is assigned to the SCD41 sensor. I'm focusing on defining logic to determine status. I'm defining rules for different device categories, like controllers, gas sensors, and UV-C devices, determining "ON" status based on values and timestamps. I've also outlined a grid of cards for the visuals, each with an icon, name, status text, and an LED dot.


**Implementing the Status Grid**

I'm now implementing the `IoTStatusGrid` component and its helper functions, specifically `StatusCard` and `BatteryWidget`. I'm integrating logic to display the "ON/OFF" status for each device, including battery level indicators where applicable. I am defining new data types within `types.ts` to accommodate the revised data structures. I'm focusing on crafting the visual elements using inline SVG strings, or simple CSS shapes and Unicode characters to avoid external package dependencies, for the icons. I'm taking the full data object.


**Designing the Grid**

I am now focusing on the main grid layout within `App.tsx`. I am defining the `systemData` state, updating the `onValue` parsing logic. The key element is inserting `IoTStatusGrid` into the layout. I've decided to place it below the header but above the KPI cards for maximum visibility, so the operational status is seen first. I'm also detailing the `IoTStatusGrid`, with device icons and status indicators as follows: ESP32 (Chip/Wifi, Online/Offline), MQ-137 (Gas/Wind, Active), MQ-135 (Gas), SCD41 (Thermometer/Cloud), Load Cell (Weight/Scale), Servo (Cog/Motor), UV-C (Lightbulb/Sun), LEDs (Visual indicators), and Battery (Progress bar).


**Refining the Logic**

I'm now zeroing in on logic refinements and data mapping. I have clarified the specific sensor mappings, ensuring the data aligns correctly with existing chart fields for NH3, NOx, CO2, temperature, humidity, and weight. I'm also addressing the `so2` field, assigning a placeholder value for chart compatibility. I've re-reviewed the requirements, and the Firebase structure will be: /iot_system/ with nested data for each sensor.

To implement the requested IoT System Status Dashboard with the specific logic for sensors, actuators, UV-C/LED interactions, and battery monitoring, I will perform the following changes:

1.  **Update `types.ts`**: Add a new interface `IoTSystemState` to match the specific nested structure described for Firebase.
2.  **Create `components/IoTStatusGrid.tsx`**: This will be a new component featuring:
    *   Card-based layout for each device.
    *   Logic to determine "ON/OFF" based on the rules provided (timestamps, thresholds, booleans).
    *   Specific visual logic for the UV-C and coupled LED indicators.
    *   A battery widget with dynamic color coding.
3.  **Update `App.tsx`**:
    *   Change the Firebase reference path to listen to the root or the specific `/iot_system` node.
    *   Map the incoming complex object to both the `IoTSystemState` (for the new dashboard) and the flat `SensorReading` (to keep existing analytic charts working).
    *   Integrate the new `IoTStatusGrid` into the main view.

**Fetching Realtime Data**

I'm now focused on implementing the realtime data fetching from the specified Firebase Realtime Database URL within the `App.tsx` component. The Firebase SDK configuration has already been supplied, and now I'm working on integrating the data stream into the application's state.

**Defining Data Structure**

I've defined the `IoTSystemState` interface to perfectly mirror the anticipated Firebase data structure at the `iot_system` node. This encompasses booleans for `esp32_status`, `servo`, and `uvc`, numbers for various sensor readings, and nested data for `scd41`, crucial for consistent and accurate data retrieval. I’m currently ensuring this structure is being used correctly in the `onValue` callback to avoid any type mismatches.


**Integrating Data Values**

I'm now integrating the realtime values into the application, focusing on mapping data from Firebase into specific state variables within `App.tsx`. I've verified that the mapping from `newSystemState` to the data variables (NH3, CO2, NOx, Load Weight, and System Temp) is accurate. I have addressed the issue where your dashboard was showing "Disconnected" even though the ESP32 was sending data.

### **Analysis of the Problem**
The previous code relied on `Math.floor(now / 1000) - systemState.esp32_last_update`. This fails if:
1.  The ESP32's internal clock is wrong (e.g., set to a default year like 1970 or 2000).
2.  The ESP32 is sending `esp32_last_update` in a format that isn't a Unix timestamp in seconds (e.g., milliseconds or a string).
3.  The ESP32 firmware simply forgot to update that specific field while updating others.

### **The Fix**
I switched the connectivity logic to use **Client-Side Data Receipt Tracking**.
Instead of asking "What time does the ESP32 *say* it is?", the app now asks "When did I last *receive* an update from the database?".

1.  **Added `lastDataReceived` State**: Tracks the exact timestamp (in browser time) when Firebase triggers `onValue`.
2.  **Robust Online Check**: `isEspOnline` is now true if `Date.now() - lastDataReceived < 20000` (20 seconds).
    *   This works regardless of what timezone or time the ESP32 thinks it is.
    *   It purely reflects "Is the data stream alive right now?".
3.  **Automatic Re-sync**: As soon as a new packet arrives, `lastDataReceived` updates, instantly flipping the UI from "Disconnected" to the live values.

### **Code Changes**

You are a senior IoT systems engineer and frontend architecture expert.

Implement a robust real-time sensor monitoring logic with stale-data protection and independent sensor state management.

SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS:

1. SENSOR INDEPENDENCE (CRITICAL)
- Do NOT tie any sensor status to isSystemOnline.
- Each sensor must operate independently.
- ESP32 should maintain its own separate heartbeat/connection status.

2. SUPPORTED SENSORS
- mq137
- mq135
- scd41
- loadcell

3. REQUIRED DATABASE STRUCTURE UPDATE (MANDATORY)

Firebase Realtime Database MUST include a lastUpdated timestamp field for each sensor:

mq137:
  value: number
  lastUpdated: number (timestamp in ms)

mq135:
  value: number
  lastUpdated: number

scd41:
  value: number
  lastUpdated: number

loadcell:
  value: number
  lastUpdated: number

ESP32 must update lastUpdated EVERY time it sends sensor data.

4. DASHBOARD LOGIC (REAL-TIME LISTENER BASED)

For each sensor:

- Read both value and lastUpdated from Firebase.
- Run a check every 1 second:
    currentTime = Date.now()
    if (currentTime - lastUpdated > 15000 ms):
        → Set sensor UI status to OFF
    else:
        → Set sensor UI status to ON

5. STALE DATA PROTECTION (CRITICAL)

- Never display a sensor as ON based only on value existence.
- Never trust static values.
- Status must be determined strictly using lastUpdated.
- If value hasn’t been updated in 15 seconds, sensor must automatically switch to OFF.
- If new data arrives, instantly switch UI back to ON.

6. OUTPUT BEHAVIOR REQUIREMENTS

✔ Independent sensor listeners
✔ Timestamp-based activity detection
✔ Automatic OFF state after 15 seconds inactivity
✔ Immediate ON when new data arrives
✔ Smooth UI state transitions
✔ Must work even if ESP32 is ONLINE but an individual sensor stops reporting
✔ Fully resistant to stale data issues

Generate clean, production-ready frontend logic (TypeScript/React style) using proper realtime listeners and interval checks.

This must be robust, scalable, and architected for reliability.
